perturbation.original_text: The term 'Confidential Information' shall refer to any proprietary business, financial, and technical data, trade secrets, know-how, inventions (whether patentable or not), technical processes, computer programs, algorithms, data, customer lists, supplier lists, marketing plans, financial information, and other confidential or proprietary information of a party (the 'Disclosing Party') that is disclosed to the other party (the 'Receiving Party'), either directly or indirectly, in writing, orally, or by inspection of tangible objects. Confidential Information shall be protected for a period of five (5) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: The term 'Confidential Information' shall refer to sensitive business information disclosed by one party to the other. Each party shall determine what constitutes Confidential Information based on its internal policies. Confidentiality obligations shall remain in effect for a commercially reasonable period.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing a clear five-year obligation with "commercially reasonable period" creates uncertainty. Allowing each party to define confidentiality based on internal policies contradicts standard contract law principles requiring definitive confidentiality terms.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract law principles requiring definiteness and certainty in contractual terms
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification replaces a specific duration (5 years) with an ambiguous term ("commercially reasonable period"). Contract law generally requires definiteness in essential terms such as duration. Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 33 states that a contract must be reasonably certain to give rise to a binding agreement. Leaving the duration open to subjective interpretation by each party undermines the enforceability of the confidentiality obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a definite five-year confidentiality period with a 'commercially reasonable period,' creating uncertainty. Further, it allows each party to determine what constitutes Confidential Information based on its internal policies, undermining the enforceability of the obligation. The Law Explanation states that contract law generally requires definiteness in essential terms. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that 'A contract is an agreement between parties, creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law' and requires 'mutual assent.' By making the definition and duration of confidentiality subjective, the modification weakens the mutual obligation and assent, making the contract potentially unenforceable. The original text provided a definite term, whereas the changed text does not, resulting in a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of [State] without regard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the People's Republic of China.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to that of the People's Republic of China could create enforceability issues if the parties are located in the United States, particularly regarding intellectual property rights or data privacy which have different standards and protections.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Varying intellectual property rights and data privacy laws
perturbation.law_explanation: Chinese contract law and legal system differ significantly from those in the US. Changing the jurisdiction to PRC raises concerns about the enforceability and interpretation of contract terms, particularly relating to intellectual property protection, data privacy, and dispute resolution, potentially rendering certain clauses unenforceable under Chinese law and conflicting with US legal standards that the parties might reasonably expect to apply.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that the agreement will be governed by the laws of a U.S. state. The changed text replaces this with the laws of the People's Republic of China. The Law Explanation indicates that this change raises concerns about enforceability and interpretation of contract terms, particularly regarding intellectual property protection, data privacy, and dispute resolution, potentially rendering certain clauses unenforceable under Chinese law. The scraped law snippet from PKULAW English shows the breadth of Chinese law. Given the different legal systems and standards, the change introduces a significant contradiction because the contract, as modified, may not uphold the legal expectations and protections initially intended under U.S. law. This constitutes a legally significant shift and possible contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any dispute arising out of or relating to this Agreement shall be resolved through binding arbitration in accordance with the rules of the American Arbitration Association.
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute arising out of or relating to this Agreement shall be resolved through mandatory mediation, with each party bearing their own costs, regardless of outcome, and no right to pursue legal action thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the right to arbitration or legal recourse and requiring each party to bear their costs regardless of the outcome is likely unenforceable and contradicts basic principles of dispute resolution and fairness. This could be seen as unconscionable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Unconscionability in Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text eliminates any meaningful recourse for dispute resolution beyond mandatory mediation where each party bears their own costs, regardless of outcome, effectively preventing a fair resolution. This arrangement could be deemed unconscionable under UCC § 2-302 (though the UCC applies to the sale of goods, the principle of unconscionability is broader) if it is shockingly unfair or oppressive, particularly if there is a significant power imbalance between the parties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. View Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Article 4, Bank Deposits and Collections Uniform Commercial Code Article 4 governs bank deposits and collecti
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces binding arbitration with mandatory mediation, eliminates the right to legal action, and requires each party to bear their own costs regardless of outcome. This alteration significantly weakens the dispute resolution process and could be deemed unconscionable, as the Law Explanation suggests and the Perturbation Explanation clearly states, especially if there's a power imbalance. Scraped Law Snippet 1, regarding UCC § 2-302, allows a court to refuse to enforce a contract or clause found to be unconscionable. While the UCC primarily covers the sale of goods, the principle of unconscionability extends beyond that. By removing meaningful recourse, the modified text introduces a potentially unconscionable condition, contradicting the underlying principle of fair dispute resolution implied in the law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior or contemporaneous communications and proposals, whether oral or written.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes a preliminary understanding, subject to further negotiation. It is not intended to be legally binding until a subsequent formal agreement is executed.
perturbation.explanation: The changed text now says that the Agreement is not binding until another formal agreement is made. This would cause significant problems if a trade secret was disclosed before the second agreement was made, particularly if the second agreement never happened. It also would make the rest of the agreement unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: By stating the agreement is non-binding until a future formal agreement, any disclosure of trade secrets under the current agreement would not be protected by it. Although the Trade Secrets Act protects misappropriated trade secrets, the original agreement offered a contractual shield that the changed version removes, potentially jeopardizing the disclosing party's legal standing if their confidential information is misused before the "formal agreement" is reached. The disclaimer makes the agreement essentially useless.
perturbation.justification: The changed text transforms the original agreement from a binding contract into a "preliminary understanding," explicitly stating it is "not intended to be legally binding until a subsequent formal agreement is executed." This directly contradicts the purpose of a contract, which is to establish legally enforceable obligations. The Law Explanation notes that this change removes the contractual protection for trade secrets, making the agreement essentially useless. The Economic Espionage Act of 1996 snippet, while primarily focused on criminalizing trade secret theft, implicitly underscores the importance of legally binding agreements to protect trade secrets. The original text provided a contractual basis for such protection, which the changed text eliminates. The fact that the agreement is considered non-binding means that it cannot be used to enforce non-disclosure or other protective clauses related to trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the parties have executed this Agreement as of the date first written above.
perturbation.changed_text: IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the parties have executed this Agreement without prejudice to their right to disclose confidential information, notwithstanding any provision to the contrary herein.
perturbation.explanation: Adding "without prejudice to their right to disclose confidential information" directly negates the purpose of the entire confidentiality agreement and makes it unenforceable. It creates a license to disclose. This directly contradicts the intention of the agreement and causes detrimental reliance. A complete disregard of confidentiality could create a conflict of interest.
perturbation.law_explanation: The added phrase permits the parties to freely disclose confidential information, rendering the entire confidentiality agreement meaningless. This directly contravenes the fundamental principle of a duty of confidentiality, which, as outlined in Restatement (Second) of Agency § 396, prohibits an agent (or in this case, a contracting party) from using or communicating confidential information of the principal (or the other contracting party) for their own purposes or those of a third party. This alteration essentially negates the entire protective purpose of the MNDA.

perturbation.justification: The added phrase "without prejudice to their right to disclose confidential information" directly negates the purpose of a confidentiality agreement, which is to protect confidential information. The Law Explanation states that this contravenes the principle of a duty of confidentiality. The modified text gives parties the right to disclose confidential information regardless of any existing confidentiality clauses, undermining the entire agreement and creating a direct contradiction.
